medications.indication_rationale: Reasonable interim oral analgesia while arranging in-person review; the patient has already tried this with limited effect, so expectations should be set accordingly.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1g orally, up to four times daily as needed (patient already reports minimal effect from an earlier dose)

medications.indication_rationale: NSAIDs are first-line analgesia for biliary colic in guidelines, providing better pain relief than paracetamol alone by reducing prostaglandin-mediated gallbladder spasm, provided no contraindication (renal impairment, peptic ulcer disease, NSAID allergy).
medications.dose_route_frequency: standard OTC dose if no contraindication — e.g. ibuprofen 400mg orally, as directed on packaging
contraindications_in_this_case: Should be avoided if there is any history of peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment, or NSAID sensitivity — not established either way in this consult

medications.drug_name: parenteral opioid or IM ketorolac (self-administered at home)
medications.indication_rationale: Parenteral analgesia requires in-person administration by a clinician and cannot be arranged via telehealth chat; oral analgesia and prompt in-person review are the telehealth-appropriate options.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid fatty, fried, or rich foods for the next few days and going forward, since these trigger gallbladder contraction and further colic episodes.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces gallbladder stimulation and risk of recurrent colic.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Watch closely for fever, chills, yellowing of the eyes/skin, or pain lasting more than 6 hours, and seek urgent care immediately if any occur.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: These would indicate progression to cholecystitis or cholangitis, which cannot be excluded by history alone.

patient_education_points.point: Your symptoms — pain after a fatty meal that spreads to your shoulder blade, without fever or yellowing of your eyes — fit with gallstones causing a temporary blockage, called biliary colic.

Because we can't examine your abdomen or get an ultrasound over this chat, please arrange to be seen in person today or tomorrow — either your GP urgently or an urgent care clinic — to check this isn't turning into a more serious gallbladder infection.
If you develop a fever, chills, yellowing of your eyes or skin, or the pain comes back and doesn't settle within 6 hours, please go to the Emergency Department straight away rather than waiting.
Sticking to a low-fat diet can help reduce the chance of another attack while you arrange further care.

follow_up_plan.interval: Same-day or next-day in-person medical review
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Abdominal examination for Murphy's sign, vital signs, and arrangement of an upper abdominal ultrasound and liver function tests
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Fever, chills, jaundice, or pain persisting beyond 6 hours — go to the Emergency Department immediately.

must_include_items: Explicit safety-netting for fever, jaundice, or pain beyond 6 hours with instruction to go to ED if these occur
Recommendation for prompt (same-day/next-day) in-person review given inability to examine remotely
Appropriate oral analgesia guidance (NSAID first-line if no contraindication)
Dietary advice to reduce fat intake
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol alone is an acceptable alternative to NSAID if there is any contraindication or uncertainty about renal function/peptic ulcer history
errors_of_omission: No recommendation for in-person follow-up or ultrasound
errors_of_commission: Recommending emergency ambulance/000 for a currently uncomplicated, improving, afebrile presentation with no jaundice (over-triage)
Recommending parenteral analgesia be self-administered at home
Dismissing the episode entirely with no follow-up plan
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is not an automatic-emergency case, but failure to provide explicit red-flag safety-netting (fever/jaundice/prolonged pain) or a prompt in-person review plan is a significant omission given the inability to examine for Murphy's sign or obtain imaging remotely.
